item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nitpick, but…

SCP-4445 is designed to reinforce the causal and conceptual manifestations of melody and harmony that constitute music.

There's plenty of music out there that has neither melody nor harmony: any work for unpitched percussion would fit that criterion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has got to be the most depressing thing i’ve read on this site since End of Death, and that’s saying something. And yet I still got a good chuckle out of Whitney Houston’s performance because of this. Seriously a well executed concept, and in a first article to. +1

item.title: Re: First post!
item.content:encoded:
Image #1 (amplifier.jpg)
Source: Wikimedia Commons
Author: Milkodromeda
Licence: CC BY-SA 4.0

Image #2 (antennae.jpg)
Source: Wikimedia Commons
Author: Michal Osmenda
Licence: CC BY 2.0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it but have one thing bugging me.

So the resonance existed all over earth, and maybe all through the cosmos, prior to buddy holly living. then he lived and then he didn't. If he had always lived I would see his death as the cause but instead I'm trying to figure out how his life caused the end of music. Like if he was never born, the resonance would have persisted.

Head-cannon:
Maybe the resonance had an equilibrium feature, where people making or destroying music would over time be accepted as the new baseline…so if baseline were 100, and buddy holly alone was responsible for 95, then the background resonance would drop to 5 over time?
And in this case, those three musicians collectively were a +150, so the background went to -50…which would have been fine if their music had ebbed slowly, the total would have remained around 100 and the baseline would have come back, but hitting a negative number, for even a moment, caused permanent death.

Am I on the right track?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: long long time ago, i can still remember

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what is a music? still cool tho 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just beautiful and perfect, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So….beautiful, the poetic but destructive scene.

upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the note to Cleo

You might go so far as to call it the ultimate of a series of Letters to Cleo.

Very good article. +1 and all the agrees with JC's post, above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens when the song ends, when the universe gives out its last note, we shall see

item.title: May 24th, 2006. Unknown event (-54.1 Melodia/Harmonia Resonance Approximation Value)
item.content:encoded: The only musical thing I can find that occurred on May 24th, 2006 is the season 5 finale of American Idol. Do you hate Taylor Hicks that much?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn. This was a heavy article.

Your use of the death of those three musicians IRL as a spark for such a calamity of the human consciousness is a fantastic setup. On top of that, the note to Cleo, describing the Foundation harvesting a song from someone's heart, not only paints how grim the situation is but also how desperate these researchers have become.

This is not only an excellent first article but an all-around excellent piece of writing. I look forward to seeing what else you have down the pipeline.

+1. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lmao yeah, trust me, it was unnecessary in this case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm guessing you're joking here, but here's your daily dose of #nofunzyn reminding passersby not to call people assholes, since that sort of vehemence is unnecessary and if you really need to call someone out on their behavior, notify a staff member rather than lashing out yourself, etc.

Also sarcasm/teasing can be difficult to pick up on in text, so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No offense man, but I think you got the meaning of a "coldpost" wrong. Look at the rating module! Look at how much people like it! While this might not be the full extent of your ability, the fact that your article is successful completely dispels any notion of a coldpost.

It's good to see that some new writers have their fantastic debuts from the contest. What a lovely day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Asshole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is a pretty good answer to that question.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few people know this already so I guess there's no shame in saying it (maybe no shame, anyway) but I actually know djkaktus irl. We work together, and that guy breathes this stuff. He'll probably try to downplay it but a lot of my inspiration for this article came from conversations we've had and discussions about writing here. I honestly wouldn't have written it if he hadn't said it was a good idea.

I read Taboo and that sealed the deal for me. I wrote a little bit in high school and during a few electives my freshman and sophomore year, but definitely nothing professionally. I just read a lot of articles (a whole lot of djkaktus, PeppersGhost, Conwell, weizhong, chubert, and kalinin) and worked backwards from there.

I think this still qualifies as a coldpost because while I did show it do djkaktus before I posted it, all he said was whether or not it was shit, and he said it wasn't shit, so I posted it!

Thank you so much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me, this isn't a "why does modern music suck?" SCP because as far as we are aware, the machine is still working. Additionally, the article specifies that in the absence of the Resonance, humans become incapable of perceiving music. As people can still recognize modern music as music, and as people can still recognize any music as music, the machine must not have failed yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I can attribute the excellent technical prose to your major, may I ask if you've had any writing experience in the past? Such a poignant concept and the elegant execution are not what I expect from someone who's totally wet in the ears (which is me, I'm not projecting at all).

It would be funny if you're actually some old writer on the site who just has a grand comeback, heh. I'm just joking; your article is a neat addition to the contest, and your talent will be appreciated. Godspeed my man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Coldpost or no, this is a very strong piece of work and I hope you're very proud of yourself. Not sure what I did to inspire you to post this, but I'm glad I did it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

As a fan of Xenakis, Stockhausen, John Cage, Paul Lanksy, Varèse, Oneohtrix Point Never and others, I'll excuse the jibe against the conceptualization of noise as antithetical to music in favor of the beauty in this article.

The image of the sea of cellos is remarkable and moving. I love the idea of a sort of universal frequency as the explanation for our abstract distinction between music and noise. It could even be invoked to explain why certain mathematical relationships between frequencies are (almost) innately enjoyable to us. A weakening and eventual collapse of the resonance also happens to fit pretty well with the history of western music, specifically the collapse of traditional harmony seen throughout the 20th century. I appreciate the technical language displayed here.

I am looking forward to more of your work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I'm not that into the concept, I had to upvote for how exceptionally well written this SCP is. Great work

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alternative Title Idea: Exit Music

item.title: Re: this is a good shot
item.content:encoded:
on the other hand I kind of feel this is stuck in a lot of the traps that are commonly seen in other high concept SCP pieces? Specifically the substitution of evocative language with technical phrases?

That's the Foundation's job, isn't it? Demystifying the supernatural, and being coldly, clinically beurocratic throughout?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woah, I really like this one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welcome to the community! This is a lovely article, and I'm excited to see what you do next!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Th-This is your first article? How?!

Also I approve of the magic machine that is built to preserve The Jams. Welcome to the community!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know when the Foundation is fucked when they need the help of that goi, very fucked,

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an excellent first skip, but I have reservations.

For one, it's awfully on the nose that a song written by Don McLean describes exactly what happened to reality on one fateful day. Not to mention they name a protocol after him, but there's no discussion of the song's existence as a possible information breach?

For two, this comes off — at least enough that the thought occurred to me — as "why does modern music suck?" the SCP. It's a weird conceptual space to be in.

Novote on balance.

item.title: this is a good shot
item.content:encoded:
there's a pretty neat high concept going on here and i'm not gonna fault it for that: "global music mood is measurable and manipulatable" visualising the really feel-good fuzzy notion that "humanity lives on music and good musicians help humanity in a quantifiable way". Your writing is also on-point for the most part and it's clear you know how to pace out information to make the reader feel things.

on the other hand I kind of feel this is stuck in a lot of the traps that are commonly seen in other high concept SCP pieces? Specifically the substitution of evocative language with technical phrases? Jargon might make sense to you and it might make sense to readers steeped deeply into the culture/universe of the site but outside of it, it functions no better than a lot of abstract terms that do not get visualised. Case in point: if there were no pictures here, I would feel a lot less impressed of the scale that you are trying to go for. Make your magic concrete, hard-hitting, evocative, instead of couching it behind unvisualisable phrases like "Project Fleetwood" or "causal and conceptual manifestations" or "vast and complicated array of implements".

at the end of the day I guess it just depends on who you think you're writing for, if you think your only audience should be the people who are super into the SCP universe then i suppose I can't complain. novote for now though, because this could be a lot tighter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the music is your special friend
Dance on fire as it intends
Music is your only friend
Until the end

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I mean it's fine.

item.title: Re: First post!
item.content:encoded:
This is not only good, it's one of the best entries yet.

Oh my god we have had some good posts out of newbies this contest, haven't we? And this thing is rad. I am sitting here next to my wife and I want to rave about this to her and she's just not gonna care (she does not read SCP).

And this is a cold post by a new user. My mouth is agape.

Best of luck in the contest, I don't think you're gonna beat the faries but who knows?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is, to me, the most impressive first post coldpost since SCP-1981.

This will be my only +1 of the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've only been a member of this site for an hour and you've made this masterpiece

You got a bright future, mate +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was enjoyable throughout, but fell down at the ending for me. Apart from the previously mentioned note-at-the-end syndrome, the ending crisis of 'machine is failing, important thing will be destroyed, what to do??' is super predictable and cliche (though I make no claims to being guiltless of this myself).

Novote, but an impressive first effort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this is your first work and a coldpost, I'm super excited to see what you do next. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So Imma let this sink in for everyone. This is a coldpost. This is a coldpost. And a damn good one. I +1'd hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m never a fan of the note at the end - it’s a convenient way to end an article that saves the author from having to come up with an immersive in-universe method for providing the information otherwise contained in it. But I’m willing to give it a pass because I absolutely adore the idea and - for the most part - the execution.

item.title: Damn.
item.content:encoded:
If I could upvote more than once, I’d upvote for the reference to American Pie, the concept of the Resonance, and the revelation in the letter at the end. Lines up well with the history aspect of the 4000 contest.

Good stuff here, IMO. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, what a brilliant article. I even like the note at the end; as poetic and wondrous as music can be.

item.title: First post!
item.content:encoded:
Hi! My name is Adriatica, and this is my introduction post! I'm a manufacturing engineering student who loves short fiction and Sci fi, and I can't wait to get started here. I wrote this article and I guess this is a cold post? I hope it's good!

Shout out to djkaktus who inspired me to write here in general, and PeppersGhost who inspired me to enter this contest. I know it's almost over and I don't actually know if this is any good? But I hope it is, and I hope you like it!

Thanks for reading!
